title: Quasisymmetric Divided Differences and Forest Polynomials
speaker: Vasu Tewari
abstract:
Postnikov's divided symmetrization, introduced in the context of volume polynomials of permutahedra, possesses a host of remarkable ``positivity'' properties. These turn out to be best understood using a family of operators we call quasisymmetric divided differences.
I will introduce these operators and then define a basis of the polynomial ring adapted to these operators in the same way as ordinary divided differences interact with Schubert polynomials. This basis works nicely with respect to reduction modulo the ideal of positive degree quasisymmetric polynomials. Furthermore the expansion of the Schubert polynomials in this basis is nonnegative-- in fact it encodes the Schubert class expansions of the classes of certain toric Richardson varieties whose moment polytopes come from a cubical subdivision of the permutahedron. I will give a combinatorial procedure to compute these Schubert structure constants, and conclude by briefly revisiting mixed Eulerian numbers and lattice point counts of permutahedra
The underlying geometry is the subject of a follow-up talk by Hunter Spink.
Joint work with Philippe Nadeau (Lyon) and Hunter Spink (Toronto).

proof_stages.label: Divided symmetrization and Schubert-positive motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines divided symmetrization, reviews several outputs it encodes, observes that the motivating inputs are Schubert-positive, and poses the problem of computing the procedure combinatorially.

proof_stages.label: Quasisymmetric coinvariant reduction
proof_stages.summary: The operator R_i is introduced to define quasisymmetric polynomials and the quasisymmetric coinvariant quotient, whose ABB monomial basis gives a first theorem: in degree n-1 divided symmetrization is the sum of coefficients of the ABB representative.

proof_stages.label: Quasisymmetric divided-difference formula
proof_stages.summary: The quasisymmetric divided-difference operators T_i are defined from the R_i, shown to kill quasisymmetric polynomials, and used in a second formula expressing divided symmetrization as an n-1 factorial sum of iterated T_i actions.

proof_stages.label: Indexed forests, trimming, and forest polynomials
proof_stages.summary: Indexed forests of interval-supported rooted binary trees are equipped with leaf-wedge trimming moves, and forest polynomials are characterized as the unique homogeneous basis on which T_i either performs the corresponding trim or gives zero.

proof_stages.label: Compatibility with the quasisymmetric quotient
proof_stages.summary: A forest polynomial reduces modulo the quasisymmetric coinvariant ideal either to zero or to itself in the ABB basis, so its divided symmetrization is zero or the all-ones coefficient sum, equivalently a linear-extension count with a hook-length formula.

proof_stages.label: Thompson monoid relations and trimming sequences
proof_stages.summary: The trimming operators satisfy exactly the positive Thompson monoid relations, which implies that a forest determines a well-defined operator T_F independent of the chosen trimming sequence, analogous to a divided-difference operator independent of reduced word.

proof_stages.label: Coefficient extraction and Schubert positivity in the forest basis
proof_stages.summary: The forest basis expansion of any polynomial is obtained by applying T_F and taking constant terms, and applying this to Schubert polynomials gives nonnegative forest expansions because T_i factors through ordinary divided differences and multiplicity-free R_i actions.

proof_stages.label: Schubert-structure and toric-Richardson interpretation
proof_stages.summary: For a forest and trimming sequence, the operator T_F applied to a Schubert polynomial is identified with a Schubert structure coefficient for an associated pair of permutations, yielding an n-1 factorial positive subdivision interpretation of permutahedral coefficients via toric Richardson pieces.

examples.name: Mixed Eulerian numbers as hypersimplex mixed volumes
examples.note: The product of initial sums with exponents c_i is fed to divided symmetrization to show that the procedure recovers mixed volumes of hypersimplices, illustrating why the operator is interesting rather than generating a proof idea.
